title: QUIC Client Certificate Acceptance Policy Work Item
summary: Add an explicit client-side certificate acceptance policy seam above the managed proof states, support a pinned leaf SHA-256 fingerprint, surface PeerCertificatePolicyAccepted, and tighten peer transport-parameter commit so commit requires proof, policy acceptance, and PeerFinishedVerified instead of proof alone.
planned_changes:
- Add a permanent client-side acceptance policy seam inside the main library and thread an optional pinned leaf SHA-256 fingerprint through the existing bridge/runtime constructor path.
- Keep the acceptance surface narrow and explicit: compare the pinned fingerprint against the leaf certificate facts already surfaced by the managed proof path, and publish PeerCertificatePolicyAccepted only when the proof state exists and the fingerprint matches.
- Default to no acceptance when no policy is configured, and route fingerprint mismatch through the existing fatal/update path rather than silently accepting.
- Tighten the commit gate so peer transport-parameter commit requires PeerCertificateVerifyVerified, PeerCertificatePolicyAccepted, and PeerFinishedVerified.
- Add focused requirement-home tests for the default-deny path, pinned-fingerprint match, pinned-fingerprint mismatch, explicit acceptance-state behavior, runtime propagation, and the tightened commit gate.
- Keep the transcript-progress owner narrow and transcript-focused; do not move orchestration into the interop harness, add a native TLS fallback, or widen the slice to trust-store integration, hostname validation, certificate-path building, revocation, 0-RTT, 1-RTT, key update, or server-role handshake crypto.
verification_plan: Run the updated CRT and RFC9001 requirement-home tests that cover the explicit acceptance seam, the pinned-fingerprint positive and negative paths, and the tightened commit gate, then run the relevant solution build/test pass for the main library and tests project.
completion_notes: The resulting slice is intentionally narrower than full handshake support. It separates proof from local acceptance, keeps the policy surface pinned to one leaf SHA-256 fingerprint, and requires explicit policy acceptance before peer transport-parameter commit can open.
out_of_scope: Certificate trust, hostname validation, identity authentication, certificate-path validation, revocation, 0-RTT, 1-RTT, key update, server-role handshake crypto, or any native TLS / QUIC fallback.
